sentences.text: So the baby was born.
And I woke up, I don't know when.
I was in the darkest corner of the corridor.
They were waiting till I will die.
And a-- a woman was standing over me, feeding me with drops of real coffee.
Where she got the real coffee-- then I knew that it is a matron.
But of course, they-- you know, that was like in the underground.
They-- then they gave each other--
--a sign that she has to help me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this.
Where she got the real coffee then, I don't know.
Anyway, they decided after a while that I'm going to survive the-- the ordeal.
No.
The baby was not with me.
The baby was premature.
And as there were no [INAUDIBLE] in those times, the nurses put him in a-- in cotton wool.
He was just wrapped in cotton wool from head to toe.
And I must say, he was a very small baby, but he was very beautiful.
He had those big black eyes, of course.
And the head-- how small he was.
His head was full of black curls.
[LAUGHS] That's how he was born.
No.
No.
Nobody--
That is what I wonder.
I can't understand, because I can still hear her voice.
And nobody ever reacted.
There were so many people around.
You know?
Anyway, I was-- when they decided that I am going to survive, they put me into a room.
I was very weak.
After a few days-- that, I have to tell you.
Those are small things which show the Polish people how difficult it was to survive in Poland, between Polish people.
A small-- the-- like the thing with the sewer.
Like the thing with the soap.
Like-- Now, it happened like that.
When they saw after a few days that I am strong enough to survive, that was a room with three beds.
So they put in the other two beds two other women who gave just birth.
And we got to-- to talk, you know, about like, you see, live in this, lie in a bed, this hospital, you talk to other.
And suddenly, during one conversation, I told the place I come from.
They sat up, both of them.
They looked at each other.
And they thought, what do you mean, the place you come from?
So I told them, I come from Lwow.
You should have seen the relief on their faces.
When I was asleep, they were wondering why I talk differently Polish than they.
Lwow had a very specific singsong way of talking, especially in this part of Lwow, Lychakiv, that was now in Poland.
That they had special expressions, they say--
And I was only two weeks in Warsaw, so I did not have time to lose this way of speaking.
I had aunts, uncles, family, everybody were-- matron was sitting in there.
And they still saw that something strange is about me.
You know?
When I explained to them that I am from Lwow, that was-- you see, they-- they accepted it, that I could talk differently, different Polish, you see?
Anyway-- What did you name your baby?
My name-- my-- they named the baby.
I was not-- I just had one look at the baby.
I thought to myself, my god.
I was black haired and was-- with-- that my looks, I am always in danger.
And now I will have to carry a baby with those black curls.
[LAUGHS] You see?
I just-- They brought me-- I did not feed the baby, of course, being so sick.
So they-- they fed him with an eyedropper, he was so weak.
And I told my Aunt Paula to choose the names if she wants to.
And she wanted very much because she wanted to name the child after her son who was killed in defense of Warsaw.
So his name was Lechoslaw, which is very Polish.
Lechoslaw.
And the other aunt wanted after her husband, Anthony.
So he got Lechoslaw Anthony Koralewicz.
And as you saw on his birth certificate, he's Anthony-- Lechoslaw Anthony Koralewicz from mother, not married, and-- and unknown father.
That is said.
We called him short Leszek.
That is very Polish.
There were some kings which were called Leszek, Polish kings.
So after two weeks in the hospital, I went home with the baby.
It was a very festive minute.
Everybody was there, the whole family and everybody.
They adored the child.
And at-- at this time, I had nothing to do with a child ever, but I wanted to bathe him, to feed him, and this.
And I was coming to my brother's.
In meantime, my brother, Henry, the younger one-- the youngest one, he was traveling.
And he made a lot of money.
You know, he was a very good businessman.
And he had a way with people, you see?
One day, he came to me and he told me a story.
There was a man coming and going from our place.
I don't know what was the connection between him and this family, but he was definitely Jewish.
And he was talking and looking.
And he wore this typical Jewish coat with a fur collar, and that.
And he was traveling very openly all over Poland.
And he approached my brother, got friendly with him.
And I told him-- told him, look.
I don't like the way that he is traveling.
With his looks, he's traveling.
He must have some genuine papers from the Gestapo.
And he-- he looks to me as a shady character.
Anyway, one day my brother comes to me and tells me, you know, that was his name-- the other man.
I don't forgot-- I forgot his-- if I knew the other.
I knew that he is [POLISH].
[POLISH] came to him with a proposition.
He had a list of very wealthy Jews who were hidden in Warsaw in different places.
And he wanted to give the list to my brother and send him there and-- to ask them for money, to blackmail them.
No.
But-- so he did not want to go because he knew those people.
So he was sending my brother.
So my brother slapped him in the face.
So I told him, look.
That is not going to end here.
I have some very bad premonition.
And I told him better you have enough money.
Everybody thinks that you are my friend and not-- that you take care so much of me, and I look much younger than he did.
He was tall and that.
So we are going to take a flat.
And we will be different in the flat.
You and I and the child, a family.
And you will go to Przemysl.
You will bring my parents-- our parents-- and my husband.
And we will stick them into the flat.
And we will be different.
He told me, all right.
We will do this, but I have to go on one trip more.
Because I have some obligation here, there, you see?
And he went.
He went, and he did not leave me a thing.
Not money, not pieces of jewelry.
Nothing.
Anyway, he told me we will have everything when I come back.
Well, he never came back.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after-- I don't know how long it was.
One night-- it was the 1st of November, 1943-- at 2 o'clock in the morning, I was woken up by the light shining into my eyes.
When I opened my eyes, there is a Gestapo man standing with a torch shining into my eyes like this, and told me to get dressed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister, my brother-in-law, my-- this, they were all dressed, as were the Prowiszaks, pile and this, this.
And they picked us all up and took us.
First, they took us to the Schupo.
Schupo was Schutzpolizei.
That was not yet Gestapo.
That was only the police.
But that was the German police.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took away my-- and that starts this story.
They took away my baby.
And they took me into an interrogating room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were stand-- standing or sitting all around.
I was standing next to the desk.
And I told them the same story.
I don't speak German.
I don't understand German.
So they had to call an interpreter.
In meantime, I could find some answer, you know, till he translated or this.
And they started to ask me questions, personal question about myself, about-- and then they started about the other people.
And you know, my sister.
And I told them I don't know them very well, we just live in the same place.
And then they started to ask me question, religious questions.
I knew a little bit what my niece told me.
But then, I told them, look.
I was never religious.
I-- what I remember, I remember from school.
It is not much.
And-- but they started to press with more and more hard questions.
And so I did a thing, which, you see, I am usually quiet.
But sometimes when I get fed up, I can flare up.
So I just looked at them and I raised my-- my voice.
And I told them, look.
How many are you here? 10, 12?
You want me to answer all your questions, each one separate questions.
And then I will answer.
They were so stunned, that they just-- this.
Forgot to talk.
You know?
And they stopped.
See?
Who could think that a Jewish woman will raise their voice to a German officer?
Not that I don't speak about Jewish, not even Polish.
But anyway, they stopped.
And by then, it was near 5 o'clock at-- and the officer, head officer, showed me the-- his watch.
And he told me, look.
It is 15 to 5:00.
If you don't tell us everything what you know about the other people, you will never see your child again.
So I was standing there.
What can I tell them?
Would I tell on my sister?
And I knew that anyway, I am-- have slim chance to go out and to see my child again.
So I was standing there the 15 minutes.
And after 15 minutes, he tells me, now what do you know?
So I told the same thing as I knew before.
I hardly know these people.
We are just by chance together under the same roof.
So he told me, you are not a mother.
You know, the German had this idea that a Jewish mother will do everything, limitless sacrifices for her child.
They called it an Affenliebe, the love of an ape.
You see?
That the Jewish mother had.
And suddenly here, there is a woman who doesn't care.
You see?
So I still did not see the child.
But he send me under guard of two Gestapo and a Volksdeutsch.
Volksdeutsch-- there were Polish people, German descendant, who signed the German nationality-- nationality at once-- allegiance, German-- at once when the German came in.
They were the Volksdeutsch.
And he was in the Schupo, the Schutzpolizei, but he spoke Polish.
He was a Pole.
So they are going to take me home.
I did not know what for, what they are going to look in home.
So here, I walk with those two German fully armed and the Polish man.
On the way home, he asked me-- starts conversation.
And he asked me, what's the name of the father of the child?
So I did not hesitate a moment.
I just grabbed the first name, Polish name, which came into my head.
And then he asked me where he's working.
So I told him the same thing, the first thing.
In-- in-- in Krakow, because my-- my-- this was in Krakow.
So then he asked me, why he didn't marry you?
So I told him, look.
It's a hard time.
When he heard that I am pregnant, he doesn't want to be burdened with a child, and he left.
He took it, and he-- we walk a little bit.
Suddenly, he tells me, something is not right here.
So I started frantically to think in my mind what I told which could not be right.
And he looks me again-- looks at me again, and he tells me, if I would be in his place, I would not leave a girl like you.
Well, my-- my heart started to beat again.
[LAUGHS] But we come home.
And the Germans started-- the two Germans started to look for everything.
I went into the kitchen to warm up a bottle for the baby.
Baby was at the-- at the Schupo.
They did not give me the baby back.
You see?
But I knew that I am going back.
It is morning.
He has to eat something.
So one German calls to my Pole, what she's doing there in the kitchen?
Of course, they did not know that I understand German.
So he tells, she's warming a bottle for the baby.
So he-- they started to laugh.
What she thinks that she will still feed her baby?
Well, I did not even move.
You know?
I finished that.
And suddenly, I saw that they are approaching a cupboard full of books.
And I knew that in between those books, there is a lot of discriminating things, special for those Polish people, you know?
They had all those literature underground.
They had those leaflets.
They had false papers for some members, you know, things like that.
So now, I was afraid not only we are going to perish, but they are going to pay for it.
So I got an inspiration.
I don't know where it come in the minute of danger.
You know?
I got an inspiration.
I went into the room.
I took out a suitcase where I had my belongings.
It was such a small place that I had to keep all-- the few things I had in the suitcase.
I took the suitcase out.
And from the suitcase, I took out a little bag.
And I put it, everything out on the table.
I don't-- and I told them-- I call them from the other room and I told them, it is-- it is that what you are looking for.
So they run from this bookcase to see what I am giving them.
There was 5,000 zlote, which was not much in those time.
There was a broken gold watch, some gold trinkets, a chain.
But it was very insignificant.
But in between, it was a very beautiful men's ring.
I must mention it because that will be the-- the end of the story.
That-- that was a ring which was only gold plated.
It was the first and last time that my brother was cheated by someone.
You know?
So it-- it had no value, but it had a beautiful shape.
So they were eagerly writing down what they took from me.
They forgot about the books and the bookcase, you know, and we left.
When we were leaving-- as I told you, we were on the fourth floor.
On the landing between two floors, the Pole told the Germans to go forward.
And he stopped me.
And then he told me like that.
He looked at me and he told, look, I am going to tell you something.
I don't care if you are Jewish or not.
And I am not going to ask you if you are Jewish or not.
But I will give you an-- one word of advice.
You are going to be transferred now all to the Gestapo headquarters.
And if you behave over there like you behaved till now, you are going to get free.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we went.
So when we come to the Schupo, they really loaded us on a truck.
Chained.
With the baby.
Now they gave me the baby back.
They put us on a truck.
And they took us to [POLISH] Avenue, which was a notorious place in Warsaw.
Even Poles rarely came out from there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us to the fifth-- fifth floor to a room that was a waiting room.
So there was standing my sister, my brother-in-law, their niece, who was by then 13 years old, the Prowiszaks, and the-- I with the baby.
And in come a German, the chief of this that was the-- apparently the Jewish-- this-- Department of the Gestapo, and a Pole, which was not in uniform.
He was his right hand, the Pole.
They looked-- oh, brother-in-law.
A man, you know, you can't deny that a-- a man can't deny that he's a Jew.
You see, he had his trousers down, and that was that.
So he took my-- him and my sister and the niece to the other room for interrogation.
And we were standing there.
Ah!
And he approached me.
He approached me, the German.
He started to measure my head.
He started to look at the lobe of my ear.
And then he pronounced that I am Aryan.
That I am not Jewish.
So the Pole, they knew better their Jews, you know?
He looked at me and he thought he wants to interrogate me.
So he got angry, the-- the German.
He told me, I give my head that she is not Jewish.
So they took my sister and her family to the next room.
We were standing so next to the wall.
And we could hear everything, what is happening there.
Every blow, every scream.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This-- absolutely, my sister was falling apart.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And here, I was standing and listening to all this.
When they finished with them, there was silence in the other room.
He came out, the Pole.
He told the Prowiszaks to go home.
And he told me to come in for my papers.
Good.
So I come in with the baby on my arm, and standing there.
There are the two desks.
The Pole sits here.
The German sits here.
And I, of course, don't understand what they are talking between themself.
And the German started to sign and look for everything, what he has to give me back.
Because if I am released, he has to give me back-- you know, the Germans are very, very scrupulating, those guises.
So he started.
And his eyes fell on this ring.
And his eyes lit up.
He wants the ring.
So the Pole looks at him and explained to him.
Look, if you sign her release, you has to give-- you have to give her everything back.
But if you want the ring, you sign that she is Jewish.
Then, you can have the ring.
And I was standing there, not even my eye lid, it-- not-- did not move, even.
And I was only waiting.
What will be stronger, his pride as a specialist in racial-- this-- don't know what-- what they call, science?
Or the greed for the ring?
That was my life on-- on this.
And then he decided-- Pride.
The pride is taking over.
That is not yet the end.
He has the pen poised to sign his number-- his name, when his eyes fell on my Kennkarte.
He takes it-- he puts the pen down.
He takes the Kennkarte, looks at it.
And he ask, why she has blue fingerprints?
And then I discovered what was wrong with my Kennkarte.
At the moment when I had to die.
See?
And he looked at the-- whole Poland has black fingerprints, why she has blue?
So for a moment, the Pole stood-- sat still.
And then he reached for the Kennkarte, take out-- took out from his hand, had a one look at that, and at me.
And then after a while of hesitation, he folded it, because it had three parts.
He folded it, never gave back the Kennkarte to the German, only to me.
And he explained to him like that.
This Kennkarte come from Krakow.
And in Krakow, sometimes they gave blue fingerprints.
He knew that I know, and I knew that he knows that it is not true.
No.
He suspected very strongly.
That is why he-- he asked all the time the German that he wants to interrogate me.
I will explain to you how I know.
Anyway, when I came in-- I forgot to tell you, that was-- shook me so much.
In front of the German were the two Kennkarte of my sister and my brother-in-law crossed with red ink.
And all the teeth of my sister on her Kennkarte lying there, see?
So that I knew that they are finished.
But anyway, how I knew for sure what it was.
I met, walking the child once in the street of Warsaw.
I had to walk with him everyday for hours because the doctor told he's premature.
He has to be outside.
I could not tell them that I am afraid to walk for hours in the street.
I had to go.
So once, I am walking in the street, and who stands at the tram stop?
The sister of my sister-in-law under false papers as well.
And we met from time to time, in-- in a church or somewhere, this.
So when-- after the story with the Gestapo, when I was released and I come home, back, I met her.
And I told her the story.
First of all, to warn her that it will not be very safe for us to meet again, you know, because this.
To-- to tell her, I told her the story.
And she told me that her-- she was working as a housekeeper in a German household.
And this woman, the-- this woman, or some other woman around, has a friend on the Gestapo.
And she will try to ask, because I was dying to know what happens to my sister.
She will try to get to know.
And the friend was just the Pole who was there.
And he told her they either were shot on the spot in the cellars of the headquarters.
But I know that she is Jewish.
But she impressed me so with her behavior that I helped her in the last moment.
That was his answer.
And I remembered what the Schupo told me on the landing.
If you behave the same way, you are going to get out.
And I did.
So I went home.
Of course, the tragedy is-- I-- first of all, I wrote straight to my husband, with whom I was still in contact.
He was hidden.
No.
The ghetto was finished by then.
It was liquidated.
He was hidden by a Polish family.
And I wrote there, and I wrote him as much as I could.
But he understand what happened.
And I wrote him that it is better that he changes his name because this Jew, this [GERMAN], which I suspected then-- I did not-- I was not sure, but I suspected only that he-- he denounced us.
That he knew his address, so better you change your place, and-- what he did, which he did.
And from this time on, we were not in touch anymore.
That was one-- more than one and a half year till the end of the war.
No.
Yes.
He knew about the-- even my parents knew he's--
At this stage, my parents did not live anymore because--
Yes.
I heard afterwards that in September, the-- the ghetto in Przemysl was liquidated, and they were taken.
Probably to Auschwitz, see--
--like everybody else at this time.
